Home | Papers

Efficient Retrieval-Augmented Summarization of Academic Papers

Jane Doe, John Smith — April 2026

Abstract

We present a retrieval-augmented summarization method that significantly improves factual consistency over standard encoder-decoder models. Extensive experiments show a 12% ROUGE-L improvement on the arXiv-Summ benchmark while reducing the latency by 35%.

1. Introduction

Automatic summarization of long scholarly documents remains an open challenge due to the hierarchical structure of arguments, dense citations, and extensive use of mathematical formulas. Recent retrieval-augmented generation models have shown promising results in open-domain question answering, but their application to academic summarization has been limited.

In this paper we introduce RAS-Paper, a hybrid architecture that combines dense passage retrieval with a fine-tuned summarization decoder. Our contributions are threefold:

  • A new retrieval index optimized for scholarly documents.
  • A two-stage training recipe that mitigates hallucination.
  • A systematic evaluation on four benchmark datasets.

2. Method

Our pipeline consists of three components: a retriever R, a re-ranker K, and a summarizer S. Given an input paper p, we first retrieve the top-k relevant passages from an indexed corpus, then re-rank them using a cross-encoder, and finally generate the summary.

3. Experiments

We evaluate on four benchmarks: arXiv-Summ, PubMed, BigPatent, and GovReport.

3.1 Datasets

  1. arXiv-Summ — 215k pairs.
  2. PubMed — 133k pairs.
  3. BigPatent — 1.3M pairs.
  4. GovReport — 19k pairs.

4. Conclusion

We demonstrated that retrieval augmentation, when paired with careful training, delivers substantial gains in both faithfulness and efficiency for academic summarization.

References

  1. Smith et al., Neural Summarization at Scale, NeurIPS 2024.
  2. Doe et al., Dense Retrieval for Long Documents, ACL 2025.
